response:
The fare structure depends on the total distance traveled by the taxi in this city. When the distance is within four kilometers the cost remains fixed at ten yuan. For any distance exceeding four kilometers an extra two yuan is added per kilometer. The spring season brings many travelers to the airport and this affects the demand for rides. The relationship between fare $\eta$ and distance $\xi$ can be written as a piecewise function. If $\xi$ is less than or equal to four then $\eta$ equals ten yuan. If $\xi$ is greater than four then $\eta$ equals ten plus two times the difference between $\xi$ and four. This formula applies even when fractions of a kilometer are rounded up to the next whole number.

A passenger paid thirty-eight yuan for a trip that covered fifteen kilometers of actual driving. We must calculate the equivalent distance caused by stopping time to find the maximum wait. The base fare for fifteen kilometers without stops would be ten plus two times eleven which is thirty-two yuan. The difference between the paid fare and the base fare is six yuan. This six yuan difference corresponds to three extra kilometers of charged distance due to waiting. Since five minutes of stopping time equals one kilometer of driving distance the total stop time is fifteen minutes. The spring weather often causes traffic delays that increase the waiting time for drivers. Thus the taxi stopped for at most fifteen minutes during this specific journey.
